roadmap.description: Families will begin their adventure by looking for signs of water and life around them in the park. This initial exploration will spark curiosity about aquatic environments and the creatures that call them home. We'll introduce the concept of wetlands as special places where land and water meet, and how they are vital for many plants and animals. The goal is to get everyone excited about discovering the magic of these natural filters. This hands-on observation helps connect abstract concepts to real-world examples within the beautiful setting of Wuhan Garden Expo Park.
roadmap.learningObjective: Identify key features of a wetland environment and understand its general importance.
steps: Gather your 'Nature Compass' handbook and supplies.
Read 'Welcome to the Wetland Wonders!' on Page 5.
Look around the park's water features. What do you see? What do you hear?
Discuss with your family: What makes this place special?
Imagine: If this was a wetland, what animals might live here?
backgroundInfo: Wetlands are areas where water covers the soil, or is present either at or near the surface of the soil all year or for varying periods of time during the year, including during the growing season.
They are often called 'nature's kidneys' because they filter pollutants from water, improving water quality.
Globally, wetlands cover about 6% of the Earth's land surface but are home to 40% of all plant and animal species.
Wuhan, known as the 'City of a Hundred Lakes,' has a rich history intertwined with wetlands and water bodies, making the Garden Expo Park an ideal location to explore these concepts.
Different types of wetlands include marshes, swamps, bogs, and fens, each with unique characteristics and biodiversity.
Wetlands also act as natural buffers against floods and storms, absorbing excess water and protecting coastal areas.
teachingTips: Encourage open-ended questions about observations, such as 'What do you notice about the water here?' or 'What sounds do you hear near the water?'
Use descriptive language to evoke imagination and wonder about the environment.
Point out any visible water plants, insects, or birds to connect observations to the concept of wetland life.

roadmap.description: Now that families are engaged, they will actively explore the park's water features more closely. They'll become 'ecosystem engineers' for a day, observing how different elements like plants, rocks, and water interact. The scavenger hunt will guide them to look for specific components that mimic a wetland's structure and function, helping them understand the roles these parts play in a healthy ecosystem. This hands-on observation builds foundational knowledge for their design challenge and encourages keen observation skills.
roadmap.learningObjective: Observe and identify various components (plants, water, soil, animals) within a water-rich environment and infer their functions.
steps: Turn to 'Ecosystem Engineer Scavenger Hunt' on Page 6 of your handbook.
Find at least three different types of plants growing near water. Draw them in your handbook.
Look for signs of animal life (birds, insects, fish, footprints, sounds).
Observe how the water moves and where it collects in different areas.
Discuss with your family: How do these parts work together? What would happen if one part was missing?
backgroundInfo: Ecosystem engineers are organisms that create, significantly modify, maintain, or destroy a habitat. Beavers building dams are classic examples, but even plants can be engineers.
Plants in wetlands (like reeds, cattails, water lilies) help stabilize soil, filter water, and provide shelter and food for animals.
Water flow is crucial; slow-moving water allows sediments to settle and nutrients to be absorbed by plants, which is key for filtration.
Microorganisms in wetland soils play a vital role in breaking down pollutants and recycling nutrients, acting as tiny clean-up crews.
The diverse animal life, from insects to birds, contributes to the food web and nutrient cycling within the wetland, supporting its overall health.
The depth and movement of water influence the types of plants and animals that can thrive in a particular wetland, leading to diverse microhabitats.
teachingTips: Guide children to use all their senses (sight, sound, smell) during their observations.
Ask 'why' and 'how' questions to encourage deeper thinking, such as 'Why do you think these plants grow here?'
Relate observations back to the idea of 'engineering' nature, highlighting how natural elements build and maintain the ecosystem.

roadmap.description: After observing, it's time to consolidate knowledge. Families will use their handbook to learn about the essential components of a healthy wetland and their specific functions. This phase connects their observations from the park to scientific concepts, explaining how plants filter water, how different soil layers work, and why biodiversity is important. They'll begin to understand the 'blueprint' of a wetland, preparing them to design their own model with a solid foundation of knowledge.
roadmap.learningObjective: Describe the main components of a wetland (plants, water, soil, animals) and explain their roles in maintaining a healthy ecosystem.
steps: Find a comfortable spot to sit and open your handbook to 'Wetland Blueprint Basics' on Page 7.
Read about the different parts of a wetland: water, plants, soil, and animals.
Discuss the 'Did You Know?' facts and answer the comprehension question together.
Match the wetland components with their functions (e.g., plants filter, soil holds water, animals contribute to the food web).
Think about how these parts are like ingredients in a recipe for a healthy wetland, each playing a crucial role.
backgroundInfo: Water: The defining feature of a wetland, providing habitat and transport for nutrients. Water can be fresh, brackish, or saline, influencing the types of life it supports.
Hydrophytic Plants: Plants adapted to grow in water or wet soil, such as cattails, water lilies, and cypress trees. They absorb excess nutrients, stabilize soil, and provide shelter.
Hydric Soils: Soils that are saturated with water for long periods, leading to anaerobic (low oxygen) conditions. These soils are crucial for nutrient cycling and storing carbon.
Biodiversity: Wetlands support a vast array of life, including insects, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals, all playing roles in the ecosystem's balance and food web.
Filter System: Wetland plants and soils remove pollutants like nitrogen, phosphorus, and heavy metals from water, acting as natural purification systems.
Carbon Sinks: Wetlands store large amounts of carbon in their soils and vegetation, helping to mitigate climate change by removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.
teachingTips: Use analogies (e.g., wetland as a sponge, plants as filters, soil as a nutrient bank) to make complex concepts relatable.
Encourage children to draw or doodle the components as they learn to aid memory and understanding.
Relate the information back to their observations in the park, reinforcing the connection between theory and real-world examples.

roadmap.description: This is the core engineering and design challenge! Families will apply their newfound knowledge to design and build a miniature wetland model using provided materials (or collected natural items). They'll need to consider how to incorporate water flow, plant placement, and habitat creation. This hands-on activity fosters creativity, problem-solving, and spatial reasoning, allowing them to truly become 'ecosystem engineers' and see their ideas come to life.
roadmap.learningObjective: Design and construct a simple wetland model that demonstrates understanding of its key components and their functions.
steps: Turn to 'My Future Wetland Model' on Page 8 of your handbook.
Brainstorm ideas for your wetland model. What problem will it solve? (e.g., filter dirty water, create a home for frogs, slow down rainwater).
Sketch your design on Page 9, thinking about where water will go, where plants will grow, and where animals might hide.
Gather your materials (tray, soil, pebbles, small plants/leaves, water, etc.) from your toolkit and collected items.
Construct your wetland model, carefully placing each component according to your design.
Add water to test your design. Does it hold water? Does it flow as intended? Does it look like a real wetland?
backgroundInfo: Design Thinking: The process involves empathizing, defining, ideating, prototyping, and testing. Here, children empathize with nature, define a wetland's needs, ideate solutions, prototype a model, and will test it.
Material Properties: Different materials (sand, pebbles, soil, clay) have varying permeability and water retention, crucial for effective wetland design and filtration.
Scale Models: Creating a small-scale model helps engineers visualize and test designs before building large-scale projects, allowing for adjustments and improvements.
Biomimicry: Designing solutions inspired by nature. Wetlands themselves are a natural solution for water purification, flood control, and habitat creation.
Water Flow Management: In a wetland, designing for slow, meandering water flow maximizes filtration and interaction with plants and soil, enhancing its cleaning power.
Habitat Creation: Incorporating diverse elements like shallow areas, deeper pools, and varied plant types creates microhabitats for different species, promoting biodiversity.
teachingTips: Emphasize that there's no 'right' answer; encourage experimentation and creative problem-solving.
Ask guiding questions: 'What will happen if you put the plants here?', 'How can you make water flow slower through your model?'
Focus on the process of design and problem-solving, celebrating effort and ingenuity, not just the final product's perfection.

roadmap.description: Now it's time to put their engineering skills to the test! Families will evaluate their wetland models by adding a small amount of 'polluted' water (e.g., water with a bit of dirt or food coloring) and observing how their design handles it. They'll discuss what worked well, what could be improved, and how their model mimics a real wetland's functions. This critical thinking phase reinforces the learning objectives and encourages iterative design, a key engineering principle.
roadmap.learningObjective: Evaluate the effectiveness of their wetland model in terms of water retention and basic filtration, and propose improvements.
steps: Turn to 'Wetland Model Test & Improve' on Page 10.
Carefully pour a small amount of 'polluted' water (water mixed with a pinch of dirt or food coloring) into one end of your model.
Observe what happens. Does the water flow through? Does it look cleaner on the other side? Where does the dirt or color go?
Discuss with your family: What parts of your wetland model worked best? What could be improved to make it more effective?
Record your observations and improvement ideas on Page 11 of your handbook.
backgroundInfo: Filtration: Wetland plants and soil act as natural filters, trapping sediments and absorbing excess nutrients. The longer water stays in contact with these elements, the cleaner it becomes.
Sedimentation: As water slows down in a wetland, heavier particles (sediments) settle to the bottom, physically removing pollutants from the water column.
Absorption: Plants take up dissolved nutrients (like nitrates and phosphates) from the water, using them for growth and preventing them from polluting downstream ecosystems.
Bioremediation: Microbes in wetland soils can break down certain chemical pollutants into less harmful substances, contributing to natural purification.
Iterative Design: Engineers rarely get it perfect on the first try. Testing, evaluating, and refining designs based on results is a crucial part of the engineering process.

Real-world Application: Constructed wetlands are used globally to treat wastewater, manage stormwater runoff, and restore degraded landscapes, demonstrating the practical application of these principles.
teachingTips: Focus on the learning from 'failures' or areas for improvement, emphasizing that mistakes are opportunities to learn and innovate.
Encourage children to articulate *why* they think something worked or didn't work, fostering analytical thinking.
Connect the model's function back to real wetlands in the park or elsewhere, reinforcing the real-world relevance of their design.

roadmap.description: To conclude the workshop, families will have a chance to share their wetland models and explain their design choices. This sharing fosters communication skills and allows children to articulate their learning journey. It's a moment to celebrate their creativity and problem-solving efforts as 'ecosystem engineers' and reflect on the importance of protecting these vital natural spaces for the future.
roadmap.learningObjective: Communicate their wetland design choices and reflect on the importance of wetlands.
steps: Turn to 'My Wetland Story' on Page 12.
Take turns explaining your wetland model to your family. What did you design? Why? What problem did it solve?
Discuss: What was the most surprising thing you learned today? How can we help protect real wetlands in our community?
Complete the reflection questions on Page 13 of your handbook.
Fill out your 'Ecosystem Engineer' certificate on Page 14 to celebrate your achievement!
backgroundInfo: Communication in STEM: Clearly explaining designs, findings, and the reasoning behind them is a critical skill for scientists and engineers.
Environmental Stewardship: Understanding the value of wetlands encourages responsible actions to protect them and promotes a sense of care for nature.
Community Impact: Protecting local wetlands benefits not only wildlife but also human communities by providing clean water, flood control, and recreational opportunities.
Global Importance: World Wetlands Day is celebrated annually on February 2nd to raise awareness about the vital role of wetlands for people and the planet.

Wuhan's Wetlands: Wuhan is famous for its numerous lakes and wetlands, which are crucial for the city's ecological health and biodiversity, including acting as important stopovers for migratory birds.

Future Engineers: By engaging in design challenges and problem-solving activities, children develop critical thinking and innovation skills essential for future STEM careers and real-world challenges.
teachingTips: Praise effort, creativity, and the process of learning, rather than focusing solely on the 'perfect' outcome.
Encourage active listening among family members as each person shares their wetland story.
Reinforce the idea that everyone can be an 'ecosystem engineer' in their daily lives by making environmentally conscious choices.

handbook.title: Nature Compass: Designing Future Wetlands
handbook.contentPrompt: Nature Compass: Designing Future Wetlands. Become an Ecosystem Engineer!
handbook.visualPrompt: A vibrant illustration of a happy family (parent and two children, ages 6-8) exploring a lush wetland. The children are pointing at water lilies and reeds, holding a handbook. The background features a serene water body with diverse wetland plants and a few birds. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Full-page illustration with title at the top and subtitle below, centered.

handbook.title: Table of Contents
handbook.contentPrompt: Page 1: Welcome! Your Wetland Adventure Begins! Page 2: Table of Contents Page 3: Safety First! Page 4: Your Ecosystem Engineer Toolkit Page 5: ENGAGE - Welcome to the Wetland Wonders! Page 6: EXPLORE - Ecosystem Engineer Scavenger Hunt Page 7: EXPLAIN - Wetland Blueprint Basics Page 8: ELABORATE - My Future Wetland Model (Part 1: Design) Page 9: ELABORATE - My Future Wetland Model (Part 2: Build) Page 10: EVALUATE - Wetland Model Test & Improve (Part 1: Test) Page 11: EVALUATE - Wetland Model Test & Improve (Part 2: Improve) Page 12: SHARE & CELEBRATE - My Wetland Story Page 13: My Wetland Reflection Page 14: Ecosystem Engineer Certificate Page 15: Thank You!
handbook.visualPrompt: A small, decorative compass icon in the top corner. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Numbered list of contents, centered on the page, with a small decorative icon.

handbook.title: Safety First!
handbook.contentPrompt: Hello, brave Ecosystem Engineers! Your safety is super important as you explore. Please remember these rules: 1. Always stay with your family and listen to your grown-ups. 2. Look with your eyes, not your hands! Do not touch unknown plants, animals, or water without asking a grown-up first. 3. Be careful near water features. Do not run or climb on slippery surfaces. 4. Stay on marked paths to protect plants and animals in the park. 5. If you see something that looks unsafe, tell a grown-up right away. 6. Drink plenty of water and wear sun protection. Have a safe and amazing adventure!
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a friendly adult holding hands with two children near a pond, all smiling. A 'No Running' sign is subtly in the background. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Text-heavy page with bullet points for safety rules. A small illustration at the top or side.

handbook.title: Your Ecosystem Engineer Toolkit
handbook.contentPrompt: Before you start your mission, make sure you have your special Ecosystem Engineer Toolkit ready! Here's what you'll need: From Home: Small plastic tray or shallow container (like a takeout container or baking dish). A small amount of soil or sand. Small pebbles or gravel. A few small leaves, twigs, or small collected plants (like moss). A small cup for water. Optional: A few drops of food coloring or a pinch of dirt for testing. Pen or pencil for drawing and writing. From the Park (collect carefully!): Small fallen leaves. Tiny twigs. Small, smooth stones. A few blades of grass or small plant pieces (only if they are already fallen or very abundant). Remember to collect only what you need and leave nature beautiful for others!
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a transparent plastic tray filled with various natural materials: soil, pebbles, small leaves, and twigs. A pencil and small cup are beside it. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Two-column list: 'From Home' and 'From the Park', with checkboxes next to each item. An illustration at the bottom.

handbook.title: Welcome to the Wetland Wonders!
handbook.contentPrompt: Wetlands are amazing places where land and water meet, creating a special home for countless plants and animals. Imagine a giant sponge that soaks up water, filters it clean, and provides a cozy nursery for baby fish and birds! That's what wetlands do for our planet. They are like nature's very own water purification systems, removing pollution and keeping our rivers and lakes healthy. From bustling marshes filled with tall reeds to quiet swamps shaded by trees, each wetland is a unique ecosystem teeming with life. They also act as natural shields, protecting our communities from floods by absorbing excess rainwater. Without wetlands, many animals would lose their homes, and our water wouldn't be as clean. So, next time you see a watery area with lots of plants, remember you're looking at a vital part of Earth's natural balance, a true wonder of the world! Did You Know? The world's largest wetland is the Pantanal in South America, covering an area larger than England! Comprehension Question: What is one important job that wetlands do for our planet?
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a diverse wetland scene with cattails, water lilies, a frog on a lily pad, and a bird flying overhead. A 'Did You Know?' speech bubble is integrated. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Main text block with a 'Did You Know?' box and a comprehension question at the bottom. Small illustration integrated with text.

handbook.title: Ecosystem Engineer Scavenger Hunt
handbook.contentPrompt: Time to put on your explorer hats! Let's find the amazing parts of a wetland right here in the park. Look for: 1. Three different types of plants near water. Draw them here! 2. Signs of animal life (feathers, footprints, insects, birds, sounds). Write or draw what you found! 3. How the water moves (slow, still, fast). 4. Different kinds of soil or rocks near the water. Wetland Fun Facts: 1. Wetlands are home to 40% of all plant and animal species, even though they cover only 6% of Earth's land surface. 2. A single beaver can build a dam up to 300 meters long, creating new wetland habitats! 3. Some wetland plants, like water hyacinths, can absorb heavy metals from polluted water, acting as natural cleaners.
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a child with a magnifying glass examining a plant near water. Small icons representing plants, animals, and water are scattered around the page. A 'Fun Facts' box is present. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Activity list with space for drawing/writing. A 'Wetland Fun Facts' box on the side or bottom.

handbook.title: Wetland Blueprint Basics
handbook.contentPrompt: Every healthy wetland is made up of a few very important parts, working together like a team! First, there's the **water** itself – it can be shallow or deep, still or flowing, and it's where many creatures live and grow. Then, we have **hydrophytic plants**, which are special plants that love wet feet! Think of tall reeds, graceful water lilies, and sturdy cypress trees. These plants are like nature's filters, soaking up extra nutrients and holding the soil in place. Next, we have **hydric soils**, which are unique soils that stay wet for a long time. These soils are full of tiny helpers, like bacteria, that break down old leaves and other materials, recycling nutrients back into the ecosystem. Finally, there are the **animals** – from tiny insects and busy frogs to graceful birds and playful fish. Each animal has a special job, helping to spread seeds, control pests, and keep the wetland balanced. Together, these parts create a vibrant, living system that cleans our water and provides a home for incredible biodiversity. Did You Know? A healthy wetland can remove up to 90% of nitrogen and 70% of phosphorus from water, preventing harmful algae blooms! Comprehension Question: Name two different parts of a wetland and describe one job each part does.
handbook.visualPrompt: An infographic-style illustration showing four interconnected circles: Water (with fish), Hydrophytic Plants (with reeds), Hydric Soils (with roots), and Animals (with a frog and bird). A 'Did You Know?' box is present. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Main text block explaining wetland components. An infographic visualizes the components. A 'Did You Know?' box and comprehension question are included.

handbook.title: My Future Wetland Model (Part 1: Design)
handbook.contentPrompt: Imagine you are an Ecosystem Engineer! Your mission is to design a mini-wetland that helps our planet. What problem will your wetland solve? Will it clean dirty water, create a cozy home for tiny creatures, or prevent floods? Brainstorm: What will your wetland look like? What materials will you use for water, plants, and soil? How will water flow through your wetland? What animals might visit your wetland? Sketch Your Design: Use the space below to draw your amazing future wetland!
handbook.visualPrompt: A large blank box with a faint grid for sketching. Small icons of a thought bubble and a pencil are at the top. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Design prompt and brainstorming questions at the top, followed by a large blank space for sketching the wetland model.

handbook.title: My Future Wetland Model (Part 2: Build)
handbook.contentPrompt: Time to build your amazing wetland model! 1. Foundation: Place your tray or container on a flat surface. This will be the base of your wetland. 2. Soil Layer: Spread a layer of soil or sand at the bottom. You can make it uneven, with higher and lower spots, just like real land. 3. Pebble Path: Add a layer of pebbles or small stones. These can help create pathways for water or act as a filter. 4. Plant Power: Carefully place your 'plants' (leaves, twigs, small collected plants) into the soil. Think about where they would grow best in a real wetland to filter water or provide shelter. 5. Water Feature: Slowly pour water into your wetland. Watch how it fills the spaces. Does it flow where you want it to? 6. Habitat Homes: Add any other natural items like small sticks, shells, or moss to create tiny homes for imaginary wetland creatures. Remember: You are building a mini-ecosystem! Think about how everything works together.
handbook.visualPrompt: A simple step-by-step diagram showing how to build a wetland model in a tray: 1. Empty tray, 2. Tray with soil, 3. Tray with soil and pebbles, 4. Tray with soil, pebbles, and small plants/leaves, 5. Tray with water added. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Numbered instructions for building the model on one side, with a visual step-by-step diagram on the other side.

handbook.title: Wetland Model Test & Improve (Part 1: Test)
handbook.contentPrompt: Great job building your wetland! Now, let's see how well it works. 1. Prepare 'Polluted' Water: In a separate small cup, mix a tiny pinch of dirt or a drop of food coloring into some water. This is your 'polluted' water. 2. The Test: Carefully pour your 'polluted' water into one end of your wetland model. 3. Observe: Watch closely! What happens to the water as it moves through your wetland? Does it look cleaner on the other side? Where does the dirt or color go? 4. Record Your Observations: What did you see happen to the 'polluted' water? Did your wetland filter the water? How well? Which parts of your wetland helped the most?
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a child pouring a small cup of brownish water into one end of a completed wetland model. Arrows show water flow. A magnifying glass icon emphasizes observation. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Instructions for the wetland test at the top, followed by blank lines for recording observations at the bottom. A small illustration demonstrates the test.

handbook.title: Wetland Model Test & Improve (Part 2: Improve)
handbook.contentPrompt: Every good engineer learns from their tests! 1. Discuss Improvements: Talk with your family. What worked well in your wetland model? What could you change to make it even better at filtering water or providing a home? 2. Ideas for Improvement: Add more layers of different materials (sand, gravel)? Change the path of the water? Add more 'plants' or different types? Create deeper or shallower areas? 3. Draw Your Improved Design: Use the space below to sketch how you would change your wetland model to make it even more effective!
handbook.visualPrompt: A large blank box with a faint grid for sketching. Icons of a lightbulb and a wrench are at the top, symbolizing ideas and improvement. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Discussion prompts and improvement ideas at the top, followed by a large blank space for sketching an improved design.

handbook.title: My Wetland Story
handbook.contentPrompt: You've done an amazing job as an Ecosystem Engineer! Now, it's time to share your creation. 1. My Wetland Story: Explain your wetland model to your family. What did you design? What problem did you want to solve? What was your favorite part of building it? What did you learn about wetlands today? 2. Protecting Wetlands: Discuss with your family: Why is it important to protect real wetlands in places like Wuhan Garden Expo Park? What is one thing we can do to help? Wetland Conservation Facts: 1. Over 35% of the world's wetlands have been lost since 1970, making conservation efforts crucial. 2. Restoring wetlands can bring back lost species and improve water quality within just a few years. 3. Wuhan has several protected wetland parks, like Chenhu Lake Wetland Nature Reserve, which are vital for migratory birds and local biodiversity.
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a family gathered around their wetland model, with one child proudly pointing to their creation. A 'Conservation Facts' box is present. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Sharing prompts and discussion questions at the top. A 'Wetland Conservation Facts' box on the side or bottom.

handbook.title: My Wetland Reflection
handbook.contentPrompt: My Wetland Reflection: Today, I learned that wetlands are important because... My favorite part of being an Ecosystem Engineer was... One challenge I faced was... and I solved it by... If I could build another wetland, I would... I can help protect wetlands by...
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a child thoughtfully looking at a small plant. Decorative leaf borders frame the page. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Numbered reflection prompts with blank lines for writing answers. Decorative elements around the text.

handbook.title: Ecosystem Engineer Certificate
handbook.contentPrompt: Certificate of Achievement. This certifies that [Child's Name] has successfully completed the 'Designing Future Wetlands: Ecosystem Engineers' workshop and is hereby recognized as a certified ECOSYSTEM ENGINEER! Date: [Date] Signature: [Parent/Guardian]
handbook.visualPrompt: A decorative certificate border with illustrations of wetland plants and animals (frog, bird, lily pad). A prominent badge icon with 'Ecosystem Engineer' in the center. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Formal certificate layout with spaces for name, date, and signature. Decorative border and central badge.

handbook.title: Thank You!
handbook.contentPrompt: Thank You for Being a Nature Compass Explorer! We hope you had a wonderful time discovering the amazing world of wetlands and becoming an Ecosystem Engineer. Keep exploring, keep questioning, and keep caring for our incredible planet! Wuhan Garden Expo Park. Nature Compass Program. [Website Address]
handbook.visualPrompt: An illustration of a stylized compass with a leaf motif. Below it, a small logo for Wuhan Garden Expo Park and a placeholder for the Nature Compass program logo. WHITE background.
handbook.layoutDescription: Centered text with a thank you message. Logos and program information at the bottom. A large compass illustration.
